text: During diagnostic laparoscopy, a large fundal fibroid is visible, with normal-appearing fallopian tubes and ovaries. The surgeon inserts a myoma screw into the fibroid for manipulation, applying traction and counter traction. Vasopressin is injected around the fibroid to reduce vascularity, causing visible blanching. A permanent cautery hook and fenestrated bipolar forceps are used to identify and develop a surgical plane around the fibroid, minimizing bleeding and avoiding injury to the adnexa. The fibroid protrudes from its coverings as the surgeon dissects using robotic instruments, relying on visual feedback to assess force application.

The surgeon reapplies the myoma screw multiple times to provide traction and counter traction, uncovering the myoma as they proceed towards the uterine fundus. Once the myoma is fully separated, it is divided from its stalk using a harmonic scalpel after the robot is undocked. Hemostasis is confirmed, and the myoma is extracted through morselation, taking care to avoid injury to visceral organs or bowel. After extraction, the peritoneal covering is closed, hemostasis is confirmed, and the ports are removed under vision.
